story:
Riley Evans had never felt this thrilled. Not when she finally earned her master’s in physics after fifteen years and two failed marriages. Not at any wedding or holiday. For years she had trained, tested, and applied to Omega Properties, submitting her name every hiring cycle until the laboratory director, Julian Lord, finally granted her a phone interview—then twelve more video interviews with his staff—followed by a punishing battery of physicals. She endured a three-day trip to the lunar base and two weeks of safety orientation designed to teach her every way the Moon could kill a person. All she wanted was to dig into regolith, pick apart failed engines, and run the shining tools of a failure-analysis and materials lab.

On her first morning, too excited to sleep, she arrived early to find the lab dark and locked. Her thumbprint wouldn’t open the glass door. She thought she heard voices inside, knocked, and got nothing. At the commissary she overheard **a woman she recognized from the staff interviews—Jenny Fry, one of her technicians—**gossiping with two others about couples defecting to Zeno Corp for better quarters, and about the “new boss lady” who supposedly came from teaching and would probably vanish after day one. Riley slipped away, stung.

Back at the door, Julian let her in and scolded her for being late. He introduced her lab leads—Victor Ruiz, Reginald “Reg” Smith, and Sherry MacQuoid—who nodded without warmth. Victor left immediately. When Riley asked for a desk, Sherry said managers didn’t sit in the lab. Julian vanished before answering, and the “office” Riley had glimpsed through the glass beside the lab entrance turned out to be a break room. Reg guarded equipment like a doorman and called her “sweetheart.” Everyone checked email. No one needed her.

Days turned to weeks. She still needed someone to open the door each morning. Her duty roster went ignored. Assignments she gave were rerouted through the leads while she stood there. Meetings were a “waste of time.” Victor barely showed but swore he’d arrived before anyone and could leave when he liked. Sherry ran her area capably but shut Riley out, even hiring a tech without telling her. Reg was lazy and petty. When Riley finally submitted performance reviews and fired Reg for cause, Julian reversed it and said she wasn’t there to fire people. Be patient, he told her. They’ll get used to you.

Riley retreated to a back corner and an old electron microscope. She worked after hours to avoid fights. One late afternoon, while doing inventory, she watched Victor appear with a box, tap an invisible keypad, and slip through a wall panel. She followed before she could talk herself out of it.

A narrow stair led into a hidden room. Glass-topped caskets moved along a track. Julian stood over a patient on a table, chest open, organs arranged with clinical care. “This one’s ready to close up,” he said. “Victor, finish it. I’ll get Larry set in cryo.” Reg and Sherry emerged to help load another body into a pod. Victor reassembled the patient with practiced hands. A monitor beeped. The person was alive.

Riley stepped back, panicked. She turned for the stairs and found Sherry behind her. Reg took her arms.

“Come on, sweetheart,” he said. “We’ve got a cozy bed for you.”

A needle pricked her skin. As darkness pulled her under, she heard Victor’s calm voice: “She created a hostile work environment.”

Riley had chased a dream to the Moon—only to discover a secret beneath her lab, a program her superiors hid behind glass and euphemism, and a team willing to protect it by making her disappear.
